coredata.dc:title: Edge carboxylation-induced charge separation dynamics of graphene quantum dot/cellulose nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot (GQD)@cellulose nanocomposites possess optoelectronic properties of interest for photovoltaic applications. However, the optoelectronic properties related to the shapes and edge types of GQDs have not been fully explored. In the present work, we investigate the effects of carboxylation on the energy alignment and charge separation dynamics at the interface of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites using density functional theory calculations. Our results show that the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites composed of hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges exhibit better photoelectric performance than those composed of other types of GQDs. Carboxylation stabilizes the energy level of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the triangular GQDs with armchair edges but destabilizes the HOMO energy level of cellulose, resulting in hole transfer from the GQDs to cellulose upon photoexcitation. However, the calculated hole transfer rate is lower than the nonradiative recombination rate because excitonic effects dominate the dynamics of charge separation in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of hole transfer and electron–hole recombination in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites.
Schematic representation of the formation of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites, where the cellulose chain is connected to GQDs of different shapes and edge types.
Optimized geometries of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites and pristine GQDs with different shapes and edge types. Only one unit cell of each nanocomposite is shown.

Projected density of states (PDOS) of GQD-a-t@cellulose nanocomposites. The red and blue lines represent the electronic states of the cellulose and the GQDs, respectively, associated with different numbers of carboxyl groups. The unoccupied states of the cellulose are located deep within the conduction band (CB) of the nanocomposite and are, therefore, not shown here. The calculations were conducted in toluene. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Projected density of states (PDOS) of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites composed of pristine GQDs with different shapes and edge types. The red, green, and blue colors mark the electronic states associated with the cellulose, the surface state of the GQDs, and the edge state of the GQDs, respectively. The dashed line indicates the Fermi level. The unoccupied cellulose states are located deep within the conduction band (CB) of the nanocomposites and are not shown in these plots. The calculations were conducted in toluene. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites comprising pristine GQDs with different shapes and edge types. The calculations were conducted in toluene.
Absorption spectra of GQD-a-t@cellulose nanocomposites passivated with different numbers of carboxyl groups. The calculations were conducted in toluene.
Calculated parameters of unfunctionalized GQDs, cellulose, and GQD@cellulose nanocomposites (the calculations were conducted in toluene).
The calculated hole transfer (ht) and nonradiative electron–hole recombination (re) rates in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites (the calculations were conducted in toluene).

head.ce:title: Edge carboxylation-induced charge separation dynamics of graphene quantum dot/cellulose nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot (GQD)@cellulose nanocomposites possess optoelectronic properties of interest for photovoltaic applications. However, the optoelectronic properties related to the shapes and edge types of GQDs have not been fully explored. In the present work, we investigate the effects of carboxylation on the energy alignment and charge separation dynamics at the interface of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites using density functional theory calculations. Our results show that the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites composed of hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges exhibit better photoelectric performance than those composed of other types of GQDs. Carboxylation stabilizes the energy level of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the triangular GQDs with armchair edges but destabilizes the HOMO energy level of cellulose, resulting in hole transfer from the GQDs to cellulose upon photoexcitation. However, the calculated hole transfer rate is lower than the nonradiative recombination rate because excitonic effects dominate the dynamics of charge separation in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an emergent family of carbon materials intended for various applications, such as photocatalysis (Bokare, Chinnusamy, & Erogbogbo, 2021; Cheng et al., 2022), bioimaging (Choppadandi et al., 2021; Chung, Revia, & Zhang, 2021), solar energy conversion (Dey, Ghorai, Das, & Ray, 2022; Mahalingam et al., 2022), and optical sensing (Anas et al., 2019; Bharathi et al., 2020). Their electronic and optical properties depend strongly on their size (Moon et al., 2021), shape (Rabeie, Mahmoodi, & Mahkam, 2022), and edge effects (Zhou et al., 2021). For example, Abdelsalam, Elhaes, and Ibrahim (2018) reported that the bandgaps of triangular GQDs with zigzag edges are smaller than those of hexagonal GQDs of the same size. In particular, a significant fraction of the electron density is localized at the edges of the triangular GQDs, while the electrons in the hexagonal GQDs are delocalized over the entire carbon skeleton. Not only the geometry of GQDs but also their functionalization plays a crucial role in tuning their electronic structures and optical properties. Mandal, Sarkar, and Sarkar (2012) investigated the electronic structures of GQDs with density functional theory (DFT) calculations and found that the covalent functionalization of GQDs shifts the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) by the same order of magnitude and, thus, does not affect their bandgaps. Sk, Ananthanarayanan, Huang, Lim, and Chen (2014) investigated the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism in GQDs using DFT and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations and found that armchair edges lead to blueshifting in the PL spectra, while chemical functionalization and point defects cause redshifting. The ability to tune the optoelectronic properties of GQDs by controlling their edges, shape, size, and functionalization makes such materials particularly useful for photocatalytic applications.

The properties of GQDs can be modified by chemical functionalization and doping (Karatay et al., 2022; Kuo, Shao, Huang, Chou, & Yang, 2018; Mahalingam et al., 2021). A common strategy for functionalizing GQDs is carboxylation, which can be achieved via reactions involving inorganic acids (Li et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2012). This acid treatment creates dangling bonds on the surface and edges of graphene oxide, which can be progressively oxidized to form hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups that are attached to the edges and basal plane of the GQDs (He, Klinowski, Forster, & Lerf, 1998). In particular, the hydroxyl and epoxy groups are the main functional groups on the basal plane of GQDs, while the carboxyl group is predominantly found on the edges (Chien et al., 2012). The presence of these oxygen-containing groups improves the solubility of GQDs in organic solvents, which is important in the preparation of GQD@polymer nanocomposites (Kausar, 2019; Nazim & Kim, 2020). Moreover, covalent functionalization significantly alters the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs, providing a strategy for developing GQD-based molecular electronic devices.

Bioderived nanocomposites with certain functionalities are promising candidates for photovoltaic devices as they represent novel materials combining the properties of their constituents to achieve improved performance, displaying, for example, excellent mechanical and optoelectronic properties, biocompatibility, and sustainability (Fleming, Gray, & Matthews, 2001; Xiong, Grant, Ma, Zhang, & Tsukruk, 2018). Natural materials, such as cotton and bacteria, can be used to biosynthesize monocrystalline microfibrils of cellulose consisting of repeating glucose units. Synthesizing cellulose from certain bacteria to obtain bacterial cellulose (BC) is notably popular because BC has a unique microstructure that is free from undesirable substances commonly found in plants, such as pectin and lignin. BC possesses many unique advantages, such as high porosity (Fu, Zhang, & Yang, 2013), high crystallinity (Huang et al., 2014), good mechanical properties (Hu, Chen, Yang, Li, & Wang, 2014), and nontoxicity (Chen, Huang, Liang, Guan, & Yu, 2013), and can serve as a good platform for biosensors.

GQDs aggregate easily in solution due to their large surface area, making their extraction difficult. Passivation of the GQD surface with functional groups, such as hydroxyl and epoxy groups, can prevent this aggregation behavior. However, a better approach to adopt for flexible electronic applications is to bond the GQDs with a matrix material, such as polymers or materials obtained via the sol–gel process. In particular, the covalent functionalization of GQDs with polymers can improve their solubility in organic solvents, consequently facilitating the processing of GQD@polymer nanocomposites in organic solvents and allowing for their separation from water-soluble precursors (Miltenburg, Schon, Kynaston, Manion, & Seferos, 2017). For example, Xu et al. (2018) fabricated a flexible microelectrode by modifying a carbon fiber with GQDs decorated with AuPd alloy nanoparticles, where the as-prepared nanocomposites showed good sensitivity to H2O2. Lv et al. (2019) incorporated GQDs into a cellulose matrix in a layer-by-layer fashion; the resulting nanocomposites exhibited blue emission and were sensitive to Fe3+. In another study, Liu et al. (2017) reported that the saturation magnetization of carbon dots (CDs) in mat-like polyaniline nanofiber (Mat-PANI)@CD nanocomposites is enhanced due to electron transfer from the Mat-PANI N-atoms to the CDs. However, these previous experimental studies have not addressed the details of the electronic structures of GQD-based nanocomposites, especially their interfacial properties, such as the energy alignment and the dynamics of charge separation. In particular, the effects of the shapes and edges of GQDs on the electronic properties of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites remain to be understood, given that the electronic structures of GQDs strongly depend on their geometry. Notably, the lack of detailed knowledge of the interfacial electronic properties of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites has inhibited the development of high-performance GQD-based nanocomposite photovoltaic devices.

Detailed information about charge separation is essential for understanding the mechanism of nanocomposite photocatalysis because the recombination of electrons and holes reduces the number of charge carriers that can participate in chemical reactions. Fig. 1 illustrates the processes of hole transfer and electron–hole recombination in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites. The process of charge separation depends on the energy alignment at the interface and the charge transfer rate (Cui, Tamukong, & Kilina, 2018). A suitable energy level alignment provides a favorable thermodynamic condition allowing the extraction of electrons and holes from the GQDs. The charge transfer rate depends on the Coulombic interaction between the electrons and holes, which is characterized by electronic coupling, and the free energy barrier for the nonradiative transition. A smaller free energy gap for a nonradiative transition decreases the potential energy barrier, resulting in a faster charge transfer rate, and vice versa. This relation is also referred to as the energy gap law. Furthermore, a flexible molecular structure accelerates the nonradiative transition. The intramolecular reorganization energy characterizes the flexibility of a molecule; the larger this energy, the more flexible the molecule.

The electronic environment determines the energy alignment of quantum dot (QD)-based nanocomposites at the interface (Kim et al., 2021; Zhou, Garoufalis, Baskoutas, Zeng, & Jia, 2022). For example, Cui et al. (2018) investigated the bonding geometries of N719 dye on the surface of CdSe QDs using DFT-based calculations and found that different bonding conformations of the dye lead to different charge transfer paths, which can accelerate or inhibit the photocatalytic efficiency of the CdSe QDs. Cui and Xue (2021) also investigated the charge transfer of N719-dye-functionalized CdSe QDs with coadsorption of D131 dye and found that the carboxylate group promoted efficient charge separation. The charge transfer rate depends on the electron–hole interaction and electron–phonon coupling (Long, Casanova, Fang, & Prezhdo, 2017; Zacharias & Kelires, 2021), where both factors are closely related to the interfacial electronic structures of nanocomposites. Due to the limitations of spectroscopy, experimentally investigating the relationship between the charge transfer rates of nanocomposites and their interfacial electronic structures is difficult. However, a theoretical approach is notably effective in obtaining detailed information about the charge transfer process at nanocomposite interfaces. For example, Long et al. (2017) studied the dynamics of electron injection from GQDs into TiO2 using nonadiabatic molecular dynamics. They found that covalent bonding between GQDs and TiO2 leads to weak nonadiabatic charge transfer that inhibits charge separation in GQDs, while π-stacking promotes ultrafast adiabatic charge transfer that accelerates this charge separation. Wazzan, Irfan, and Fagieh (2022) recently investigated the charge transfer of GQD–Ixora floral natural dye (GQD-NDIX) nanocomposites and found that their energy alignment ensures an efficient charge injection into TiO2, facilitating charge separation. These previous studies have suggested that nonradiative charge transfer in functionalized QDs depends on the explicit interaction between the QDs and the molecules bound to them.

In this study, we explore the optoelectronic properties of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites using DFT-based calculations, analyzing the variation of the energy alignment between GQDs and cellulose as a function of the edge types and shapes of the GQDs. In particular, we investigate the thermodynamic conditions for hole transfer from the GQDs to the cellulose based on the presence of carboxyl groups on the former and calculate the nonradiative electron–hole recombination rate in the GQDs and the hole transfer rate. Our results show that the carboxyl group stabilizes the HOMO in hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges but destabilizes the HOMO of the cellulose, which enables the extraction of holes from the GQDs and, thus, facilitates charge separation. However, it has little effect on the relative position of the HOMOs of the other types of GQDs and cellulose, suggesting that the effect of carboxyl substitution at the GQD edges on the energy alignment between GQDs and cellulose depends on the edge types and shapes of the GQDs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic study of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites regarding the details of their electronic structures and the dynamics of their interfacial charge separation. The results of the computational investigation reveal the key elements for the interfacial charge transfer of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites and, therefore, aid the rational design of GQD-based electronic devices.

Four GQD models of similar sizes with two different edge types (i.e., zigzag and armchair) and shapes (i.e., hexagonal and triangular) were constructed. These types of GQDs have been the subject of several experimental investigations (Choudhary, Shukla, Vaibhav, Pawar, & Saxena, 2015; Kundu & Pillai, 2020; Tian, Tang, Teng, & Lau, 2018). The model for cellulose was constructed with 6 × 1 × 1 cells, that is, (C6H10O5)6, and periodic boundary conditions were applied along the x-direction of the cellulose chain. A vacuum space of 5 Å was added on each side of the chain in the y- and z-directions to avoid self-interaction. The initial geometries of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites were constructed by connecting the GQDs to the cellulose chain through hydrogen bonding between the GQD carboxylate groups and the cellulose hydroxyl groups (Fig. 2 ; Lv et al., 2019).

For simplicity, the pristine GQDs (i.e., the GQDs not attached to the cellulose chain) are denoted as GQD-z-h, GQD-z-t, GQD-a-h, and GQD-a-t, where “z” and “a” represent the zigzag and armchair edge types, respectively, and “h” and “t” represent the hexagonal and triangular shapes, respectively. Accordingly, the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites are denoted as GQD-z-h@cellulose, GQD-z-t@cellulose, GQD-a-h@cellulose, and GQD-a-t@cellulose. In addition, we introduced notation to represent the number of carboxyl groups attached to the GQD edges (the number after the colon); for example, “GQD-z-h@cellulose:3COOH” represents the GQD@cellulose nanocomposite composed of a hexagonal GQD with zigzag edges and three carboxyl groups attached.

Three different carboxyl capping densities (i.e., COOH, 3COOH, and 5COOH) were investigated with regard to their effects on the electronic structures and optical properties of the GQDs. The carboxyl group plays a fundamental role in chemistry and biology because its high dipole moment increases the reactivity of a particular structure, a property that is very useful in many applications. The photophysical properties of the GQDs with respect to the carboxyl groups on their edges were examined, notably the energy alignment of the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) between the GQDs and the cellulose.

DFT and TDDFT are two methods commonly used to study the optoelectronic properties of nanomaterials. The solvent effect is usually accounted for using a polarizable continuum model (PCM) (Frisch et al., 2009). In particular, the combination of TDDFT with a PCM model effectively predicts the absorption and emission energies of GQD materials (Feng et al., 2019). The geometry optimization of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites was carried out using the PBEPBE functional with GTH pseudopotentials and double-ζ basis sets with polarization functions (DZVP), as implemented with the CP2K software package (Hutter, Iannuzzi, Schiffmann, & VandeVondele, 2014). Subsequently, the electronic structures of the nanocomposites were determined by conducting single-point calculations with PBE1PBE functionals. After constructing a supercell consisting of 120 unit cells – that is, (C6H10O5)120 – of cellulose, the excitation energies of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites were determined via TDDFT calculations, which sampled the Brillouin zone at the Γ-point. A total of 40 excited states were calculated, and a further Gaussian linewidth broadening of 0.08 eV was applied. The natural transition orbitals (NTOs) were obtained by performing a unitary transformation of canonical molecular orbitals (Lu & Chen, 2012). All calculations were performed in toluene, described with a polarizable continuum dielectric model (Takano & Houk, 2005).

Properties such as the chemical hardness, chemical potential, electrophilicity index, electronic conductivity, and binding energy are very useful for studying the chemical reactivity, charge transport, and structural stability of GQD materials. In particular, the chemical potential and electrophilicity index measure the ability of a molecule to accept or donate electrons, respectively. A lower chemical potential and higher electrophilicity index are desirable for the photoelectric performance of a light sensitizer (Wazzan et al., 2022) since they improve the charge transfer performance of photovoltaic devices. In addition, chemical hardness measures the ability of a molecule to exchange charges with the external environment; a lower value for the chemical hardness of a molecule increases its ability to transfer charges. The calculation details for these parameters are provided in the supplementary material.

In the present work, the GQDs are the main light sensitizers, excited by the absorption of sunlight. It is expected that the photoexcited holes in the HOMO of the GQDs can be transferred to the HOMO of cellulose upon photorelaxation to separate the photoexcited electrons and holes in the GQDs. The recombination of electrons and holes across the interface in nanocomposites – see Fig. 1, “(re’)” – is generally a much slower process than the nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes or the charge transfer at the interface (Long et al., 2017). This retardation is due to the large energy gap between the LUMO of GQDs and the HOMO of cellulose, leading to a large energy barrier for the nonradiative transition. Moreover, the large spatial distance between the GQDs and the cellulose reduces the Coulombic interaction between the photoexcited electrons and holes. Therefore, in this work, only two pathways of charge transfer (i.e., in Fig. 1, the hole transfer [ht] from the GQDs to the cellulose and the nonradiative electron–hole recombination [re] in the GQDs) were considered. This work focuses on investigating how carboxylation affects the energy alignment between GQDs and cellulose to create favorable thermodynamic conditions for charge separation. In particular, the detailed mechanisms controlling these two charge transfer processes – including, for example, electron-vibrational coupling, electronic coupling, and free energy changes – are studied, as these parameters play a crucial role in mediating nonradiative charge transfer.

In the case of nonradiative electron–hole recombination, the final state refers to the ground state of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposite, while the initial one refers to the excited state of the nanocomposite with a significant portion of the hole density present in the GQD. Regarding hole transfer, the final state refers to the excited state of the nanocomposite with a significant portion of the hole density present in the cellulose, while the initial one is equivalent to the initial state of nonradiative electron–hole recombination. The solvent reorganization energies for the two charge transfer processes were evaluated at the optimized geometries of their initial states. As discussed above, the initial and final states can be obtained by performing an NTO analysis.

The electronic coupling was obtained based on the two-state approximation using the generalized Mulliken–Hush method (GMH), as given by Eq. (9) (Cave & Newton, 1996, 1997):

The optimized ground-state geometries of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3 . The CC bond length in a GQD is 1.42 Å, in good agreement with previous reports (Budyka, Zyubina, Ryabenko, Lin, & Mebel, 2005; Wang et al., 2007). In the cellulose chain, the lengths of the CC and CH bonds in the glucose rings are 1.53 and 1.12 Å, respectively, where the CH bonds form an angle of 108.95° with adjacent CC bonds. The CH bonds of the CH2OH group form an angle of 105.41° with each other, and the length of the OH bond is 0.1 Å. Moreover, intramolecular hydrogen bonding occurs between the hydroxyl groups in adjacent rings and between hydroxyl groups and the ring oxygen of an adjacent ring. The calculated structural parameters of the cellulose chain agree with previous results of the refinement analysis of X-ray diffraction data (Cael, Gardner, Koenig, & Blackwell, 1975).

According to previous experimental studies, GQDs are connected to the cellulose chain via carboxylate groups, which are abundant at the GQD edges (Abdelsalam et al., 2018; Al-Aqtash & Vasiliev, 2009). The binding of the carboxyl groups to the GQD edges redistributes the charge between the GQDs and the cellulose, resulting in the variation of the binding energies between them. In particular, regarding the GQDs with zigzag edges, carboxylation decreases the binding energy between the GQDs and the cellulose (Table 1 ), while in the case of GQDs with armchair edges, the binding energy depends on the shape of the GQD: in the armchair hexagonal and triangular GQDs, carboxylation increases and decreases the binding energy, respectively. In addition, increasing the polarity of the solvent decreases the binding energies between the GQDs and cellulose (Supplementary Table S1) because the screening effect of the solvent reduces the dipole-dipole interaction between GQDs and cellulose (Fischer, Crotty, Kilina, Ivanov, & Tretiak, 2012).

The calculated bandgaps of GQD-a-h, GQD-a-t, GQD-z-h, and GQD-z-t are 3.83, 4.13, 3.03, and 0.40 eV, respectively, which are in good agreement with a previous DFT-based model (Abdelsalam et al., 2018) and tight-binding calculations (Zhang, Chang, & Peeters, 2008). Chemical hardness (γ) describes the reactivity of a molecule and is proportional to the bandgap. A larger bandgap results in a larger γ; therefore, the molecule is less easily polarized. In contrast, a soft molecule has a very small γ and is more reactive and unstable. Table 1 shows that the γ of the pristine GQDs with armchair edges (GQD-a-h and GQD-a-t) decreases once they are bonded to the cellulose chain, while the change in the γ of the GQDs with zigzag edges (GQD-z-h and GQD-z-t) depend on their shape. The binding of GQD-z-t and GQD-z-h to the cellulose chain respectively decreases and increases the chemical reactivity of the respective nanocomposite.

Table 1 shows that adding more carboxyl groups to the zigzag edges of GQDs in toluene increases the γ of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites. In contrast, the γ of carboxyl-functionalized GQD-z-t@cellulose in acetonitrile decreases with the increasing number of carboxyl groups at the GQD edges, especially at a high passivation density (Supplementary Table S1). Regarding the GQDs with armchair edges, the effect of carboxylation on γ depends on the shape of the GQDs; the γ of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposite increases as more carboxyl groups are attached to the armchair edges of the triangular GQD but decreases with the increasing number of carboxyls in the armchair hexagonal GQD. This significant difference in the changes in γ is related to the energy shifts of the HOMO and LUMO of the GQDs.

Adding more carboxyl groups stabilizes both the HOMO and LUMO levels of the hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges (Supplementary Fig. S1), shifting their energy levels downward. However, the energy level of the LUMO is shifted more than that of the HOMO, decreasing the bandgap of the nanocomposite and increasing its chemical reactivity. Adding carboxyl groups destabilizes the LUMO and stabilizes the HOMO levels in triangular GQDs with armchair edges (Fig. 4 ), increasing the bandgap and decreasing the chemical reactivity of the nanocomposites. In the case of the GQDs with zigzag edges (Supplementary Figs. S2 and S3), attaching more carboxyl groups stabilizes the HOMO and LUMO levels and shifts them to lower energies. However, the energy of the HOMO is reduced to a greater extent than that of the LUMO at a higher passivation density of the carboxyl groups, which increases the bandgap of the nanocomposite and, thus, decreases its chemical reactivity.

According to Supplementary Eq. (S6), the electronic conductivity (δ) is inversely proportional to the bandgap or chemical hardness of the GQDs. Therefore, attaching GQDs to a cellulose chain increases the δ, except in the case of GQD-z-t. Regarding GQDs with zigzag edges, an increase in the passivation density of the carboxyl groups decreases the δ, while in the case of GQDs with armchair edges, δ depends on the specific shape of the GQDs, similar to the chemical hardness.

The chemical potential (β) measures the ability of a molecule to attract electrons; the lower the β, the better an electron acceptor the molecule is. The ability to accept electrons improves when the GQDs are bound to the cellulose chain, suggesting that the formation of nanocomposites improves the ability to attract electrons due to the charge distribution between the GQD and the cellulose. The β values of GQD-z-h@cellulose, GQD-z-t@cellulose, and GQD-a-h@cellulose decrease after carboxylation and continue to decrease as more carboxyl groups are attached to the GQD edges. This trend indicates that carboxylation increases the ability of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites to attract and accept electrons. However, in GQD-a-t@cellulose, adding one carboxyl group to the GQD edge slightly decreases the β while adding further carboxyl groups increases the β, indicating that the carboxylation of triangular GQDs with armchair edges decreases the intrinsic ability of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites to attract electrons.

The electrophilicity index (ϑ) measures the ability of a molecule to donate electrons. Except in the case of GQD-z-t, the ϑ of the GQDs increases when they are bound to cellulose. The ϑ values of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites are associated with both the shapes and edge types of the GQDs. For example, adding carboxyl groups to the hexagonal GQDs increases the ϑ of the nanocomposites, while adding them to the triangular GQDs with armchair edges decreases the ϑ value. Furthermore, the influence of carboxylation on the ϑ of nanocomposites comprising triangular GQDs with zigzag edges depends on the solvent polarity. For example, in nonpolar solvents (i.e., toluene), carboxylation decreases the overall ability of the nanocomposites to donate electrons, while in polar solvents (i.e., acetonitrile), the ϑ of the nanocomposites increases as the capping density of the carboxyl groups increases (Supplementary Table S1), especially at the highest capping density (i.e., 5COOH). Overall, GQD-a-h@cellulose has the highest ϑ and the lowest β among all the nanocomposites when fully passivated with carboxyl groups (i.e., containing five COOH groups). This indicates that the carboxylation of the hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges improves the photoelectric performance of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites to a greater extent than that of the other types of GQDs.

The dipole moments (DMs) of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites are larger compared to those of the pristine GQDs because the binding of the GQDs to the cellulose chain redistributes the charges between them. Further, the pristine triangular GQDs have larger DMs than their hexagonal counterparts. Larger DMs are caused by the formation of permanent dipoles at the edges of the GQDs. The electronegativity of carbon is higher than that of hydrogen; therefore, it withdraws electrons from hydrogen. Consequently, local dipoles form at the edges of the GQDs. In hexagonal GQDs covered with only hydrogen atoms, these local dipoles cancel each other out such that the total DMs in the GQDs are zero. In contrast, the local dipoles in triangular GQDs covered with only hydrogen atoms are not balanced, resulting in nonzero DMs. This leads to larger DMs in pristine triangular GQDs than in the hexagonal ones. The change in the DMs with increases in the number of carboxyl groups is not systematic. For example, the DM of GQD-z-t@cellulose is highest when no edge carboxyl groups are present, while that of GQD-a-t@cellulose is highest with three edge carboxyl groups.

The addition of carboxyl groups stabilizes the HOMO levels of the GQDs and cellulose in GQD-z-t@cellulose, GQD-a-h@cellulose, and GQD-z-h@cellulose (Supplementary Figs. S1–S3), lowering their energies. The energy shifts of the HOMO and LUMO levels of GQD-a-h@cellulose and GQD-z-h@cellulose are consistent with a previous report stating that the functionalization of hexagonal GQDs with electron-withdrawing groups lowers the energies of both the HOMO and LUMO levels of the GQDs (Li, Shu, Niu, & Wang, 2015; Mandal et al., 2012). Therefore, the energy alignments in these nanocomposites still do not favor charge separation because the HOMO level of the GQDs is lower than that of the cellulose. Moreover, the energy gaps between the HOMO levels of the GQDs and cellulose in these nanocomposites show no obvious changes when more carboxyl groups are added to the GQD edges. In contrast, in the case of GQD-a-t@cellulose, adding carboxyl groups stabilizes the HOMO level of the GQDs while destabilizing that of cellulose (Fig. 4), creating favorable thermodynamic conditions for the extraction of holes from GQDs with five carboxyl groups attached. Such an energy alignment is beneficial for the extraction of holes from GQDs and reduces the probability of nonradiative electron–hole recombination.

The shifts in the HOMO levels of the GQDs and cellulose in GQD-a-t@cellulose are due to the existence of strong Coulombic interactions (electrostatic dipole−dipole coupling) between the constituents (i.e., the GQDs and cellulose chain) in nanocomposites (Cui et al., 2018; Cui & Xue, 2021). Such strong dipole–dipole interactions lead to charge redistribution between GQDs and cellulose. Adding strongly negative carboxyl groups onto the edges of GQD-a-t increases the local dipoles and, thus, the DMs of the GQDs. Consequently, further charge redistribution occurs between the GQDs and cellulose, as evidenced by the two- to threefold increase in the DMs of functionalized GQD-a-t@cellulose nanocomposites with high edge coverages of carboxyl groups (i.e., 3COOH and 5COOH). The redistribution of charge between GQDs and cellulose in nanocomposites results in the stabilization or destabilization of the HOMO levels of the constituents, similar to the case of CdSe QD@dye nanocomposites (Kilina, Cui, Fischer, & Tretiak, 2014). In particular, the carboxyl group has a strong electronegativity; therefore, carboxylation leads to charge transfer from cellulose to GQD. The increase of charge carrier density in GQD increases the screening effect and stabilizes the HOMO of GQD, while the decrease of charge carrier density in cellulose increases the electronic interaction and destabilizes the HOMO of cellulose. Moreover, increasing the polarity of the solvent destabilizes the occupied orbitals of cellulose due to the positive charge of cellulose (Kilina et al., 2014), increasing the driving force of hole transfer from GQD to cellulose.

Notably, increasing the polarity of the solvent stabilizes the surface states of QDs (Fischer et al., 2012) and, therefore, increases their bandgaps. Consequently, the γ of pristine GQDs increases while their δ decreases with increases in the solvent polarity (Supplementary Table S1). However, the binding of the GQDs to the cellulose chain and the carboxyl substitution at the GQD edges lead to a redistribution of charge due to the dipole–dipole interactions, resulting in a change in the energy levels of the GQD@cellulose FMOs. Therefore, these FMO energy levels are determined by the combined effects of solvent polarity and dipole–dipole interactions. Increasing the solvent polarity increases the γ of GQD-a-h@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives, GQD-z-t@cellulose, and GQD-z-t@cellulose:COOH due to the stabilization of the surface state of the GQDs (Supplementary Table S1). In contrast, the γ values of GQD-z-t@cellulose:3COOH, GQD-z-t@cellulose:5COOH, and GQD-z-h@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives decrease in polar solvents, suggesting that the dipole–dipole interactions play a dominant role in regulating the FMOs of these nanocomposites. The variation of γ in GQD-a-t@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives with respect to solvent polarity is not systematic, suggesting that both dipole–dipole interactions and solvent polarity play a crucial role in regulating the nanocomposite FMOs. Moreover, polar solvents (i.e., acetonitrile) generally increase the β and decrease the ϑ of nanocomposites compared to nonpolar solvents (i.e., toluene), suggesting that less polar solvents improve the charge transfer performance of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites.

A sensitizer should collect as much solar energy as possible in the visible and near-infrared regions. Here, the absorption spectra of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites composed of triangular GQDs with armchair edges are used as an example (Fig. 7 ). The absorption spectra of the other nanocomposites are included in the supplementary material (Figs. S4–S6). The main absorption of all nanocomposites occurs in the visible region. In addition, all except GQD-a-t@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives absorb light in the infrared region. Carboxylation leads to blueshifting and increases the absorption intensity of GQD-a-t@cellulose. In addition, the spectral profiles of the carboxyl-functionalized GQD-a-t@cellulose become narrower, indicating that the binding of the carboxyl groups to the armchair edges of the triangular GQDs reduces the light-collecting ability of the nanocomposites. The blueshifting in and narrowing of the absorption spectra are in direct contrast to the behavior of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites comprising hexagonal GQDs with zigzag edges (Supplementary Fig. S4), where carboxylation leads to the redshifting of the main peak in the absorption spectra without significant changes in the spectral profile. In the case of the nanocomposites comprising hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges (Supplementary Fig. S5), carboxylation leads to additional peaks in the low-energy region and decreases the intensities of the main absorption peaks. In addition, in the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites comprising triangular GQDs with zigzag edges (Supplementary Fig. S6), carboxylation causes blueshifting in the absorption spectra at a high carboxyl group passivation density (i.e., GQD-z-t@cellulose:5COOH) in a nonpolar solvent. However, no significant shifting occurs for GQD-z-t@cellulose:5COOH in a polar solvent (Supplementary Fig. S10), as explained in the discussion that follows.

One can note that increasing the solvent polarity does not change the overall profile of the absorption spectra of the nanocomposites, except for the observed red or blue shifts, as shown in the calculated spectra in acetonitrile (Supplementary Figs. S7–S10). In particular, the absorption spectra of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites consisting of hexagonal GQDs are redshifted in a polar compared to a nonpolar solvent. In contrast, the absorption spectra of GQD-z-t@cellulose and GQD-z-t@cellulose:COOH are blueshifted in a polar solvent (Supplementary Fig. S10), while those of GQD-z-t@celluose:3COOH and GQD-z-t@cellulose:5COOH are redshifted in a polar solvent. These differences arise from the association of the electronic structures of the functionalized GQD-z-t@cellulose with respect to the solvent effect and the dipole–dipole interactions between GQDs and cellulose, as analyzed above. The absorption spectra of GQD-a-t@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives are barely shifted in a polar solvent, suggesting that their electronic structures are less affected by the solvent polarity compared to the case of other nanocomposites. This is also evidenced by smaller changes in the γ, β, δ, and ϑ of GQD-a-t@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives than in the other nanocomposites in a polar solvent (Supplementary Table S1).

Overall, the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites comprising hexagonal GQDs exhibit better light-harvesting performance due to the redshifting of the absorption spectrum and the extension of the spectral response to the low-energy region. In contrast, attaching carboxyl groups to the armchair edges of the triangular GQDs decreases the light-harvesting ability of the corresponding GQD@cellulose nanocomposites due to a blueshift in the absorption spectrum. In addition, the nanocomposites comprising triangular GQDs with zigzag edges show lower light-harvesting ability when the carboxyl group passivation density is high.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge separation dynamics in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalization of GQD-a-t@cellulose nanocomposites with five carboxyl groups provides favorable thermodynamic conditions for extracting the holes from the GQDs. It is noteworthy to investigate whether this process can compete with the nonradiative electron–hole recombination in GQDs to facilitate charge separation in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites. Nonradiative recombination is the main cause of the low performance of photovoltaic devices as it reduces the short-circuit current density and leads to open-circuit voltage losses. Therefore, fast hole transfer reduces the probability of the nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes by separating the photoexcited electron–hole pairs and improving the device's energy conversion efficiency.

According to Eq. (1), the rate of charge transfer is inversely proportional to the change in free energy; however, it is proportional to the electronic coupling and the rearrangement of atoms (i.e., the reorganization energy). Higher electronic coupling (i.e., the electronic interaction between excited and ground states) accelerates the nonradiative charge transfer because it is included in the numerator term of the rate expression in Eq. (1). Significant electronic coupling in GQDs indicates a large electron–hole interaction and, thus, a large excitonic effect.

Reducing the free energy change and increasing the reorganization energy decreases the potential energy barrier for nonradiative transitions, accelerating nonradiative charge transfer (Tong, Chan, Chang, & Che, 2015). Further, the quantum confinement of the GQDs determines the free energy change for nonradiative charge transfer. The larger the size of the GQDs, the smaller their bandgap. In contrast, the free energy change for hole transfer is determined by the energy gap between the HOMO levels of the GQDs and cellulose. Since the GQD size barely affects the HOMO level of the GQDs, due to the heavier effective mass of the holes compared to the electrons, it does not significantly impact the hole transfer rate.

Table 2 shows that the calculated rates for nonradiative electron–hole recombination and hole transfer are in the nanosecond range, in good agreement with previous theoretical predictions (Cui & Xue, 2022a, 2022b; Trinastic, Chu, & Cheng, 2015) and experimental reports (Mueller, Yan, Dragnea, & Li, 2011; Wen, Yu, Toh, Hao, & Tang, 2013). The hole transfer rate is lower than the nonradiative recombination rate in GQD-a-t@cellulose nanocomposites, indicating an inefficient charge separation process at the interface between the GQDs and cellulose. The free energy change of hole transfer is smaller than that of nonradiative electron–hole recombination, while the solvent and intramolecular reorganization energies of hole transfer are greater than those of the recombination process. Moreover, the electronic coupling of hole transfer is smaller than that of nonradiative electron–hole recombination. Thus, a smaller free energy change and larger solvent and intramolecular reorganization energies lead to faster hole transfer than nonradiative electron–hole recombination. Meanwhile, greater electronic coupling leads to faster nonradiative electron–hole recombination than hole transfer. Therefore, faster nonradiative electron–hole recombination indicates that electronic coupling dominates the charge separation dynamics in GQD-a-t@cellulose nanocomposites rather than the free energy change and reorganization energy. This phenomenon is consistent with previous reports indicating that excitonic, rather than quantization, effects have a major impact on the electronic structures of smaller GQDs (Choi, 2017; Li et al., 2015; Li & Yan, 2010).

In this study, we investigated the electronic structures and optical properties of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites by performing DFT-based theoretical calculations. Adding carboxyl groups to the GQD edges affected the binding energy between the GQDs and the cellulose. Specifically, adding carboxyl groups to triangular GQDs or GQDs with zigzag edges decreased the binding energy, while adding them to hexagonal GQDs or GQDs with armchair edges had the opposite effect. Due to the localized edge states, the triangular GQD with zigzag edges had the smallest bandgap. The photoelectric performance parameters, such as the chemical hardness, chemical potential, and electrophilicity index, strongly depended on the shape and edge type of the GQDs. In particular, the GQD@cellulose nanocomposite comprising hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges showed better photoelectric performance than the nanocomposites composed of other GQD types.

Polar solvents stabilize the surface state of GQDs and increase their bandgaps. The formation of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites and the carboxyl substitution at the GQD edges lead to charge redistribution between the GQDs and the cellulose chain. Therefore, the energy levels of the FMOs in GQD@cellulose nanocomposites are determined by the combined effects of solvent polarity and GQD–cellulose interactions. In particular, solvent polarity plays a significant role in the electronic structures of GQD-a-h@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives and GQD-z-t@cellulose nanocomposites with a low edge coverage of carboxyl groups. In contrast, the dipole–dipole interaction between GQDs and cellulose plays a dominant role in the electronic structures of GQD-z-h@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives and GQD-z-t@cellulose nanocomposites with a high edge coverage of carboxyl groups. Regarding the electronic structures of GQD-a-t@cellulose and its oxidized derivatives, both the polarity of the solvent and the dipole–dipole interactions are crucial; however, the influence of solvent polarity in these materials is smaller compared to that in the other nanocomposites, as evidenced by the lack of shifting in the band positions in their absorption spectra.

The absorption spectra of GQD@cellulose nanocomposites composed of triangular GQDs with armchair edges were blueshifted after carboxylation, resulting in a narrowing of the spectral profile and reducing the ability of the nanocomposites to collect light. In contrast, the hexagonal GQDs positively affected the light-collecting ability of the GQD@cellulose nanocomposites, as the spectral response extended into the low-energy range.

Adding carboxyl groups to the GQD edges had a minimal effect on the energy gap between the HOMO levels for GQD@cellulose nanocomposites comprising GQDs with zigzag edges and hexagonal GQDs with armchair edges. A change in the relative positions of the HOMO energy levels between the GQD and the cellulose at a high passivation density of the carboxyl groups was observed. This created a favorable condition for extracting holes from the GQDs and transferring them to the cellulose. The calculated hole transfer rate is lower than the nonradiative electron–hole recombination rate in GQDs due to the greater electronic coupling associated with the recombination step.
Peng Cui: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation. Yuan Xue: Project administration.
